name: troubleshooting
context: Fix common git mistakes — undo commits, resolve conflicts, recover lost work, clean up history, and use reflog
info: This context covers the most common git mistakes and how to fix them: undoing commits (reset, revert), recovering lost commits from reflog, resolving merge and rebase conflicts, cleaning up a messy working tree, removing sensitive files from history, fixing a detached HEAD, and dealing with force-push disasters. Git almost never truly destroys data — the reflog retains every HEAD position for 90 days by default, making most mistakes recoverable. This is the go-to resource when something goes wrong. See [[git/commit]] for amending the last commit, [[git/rebase]] for rewriting older history, [[git/merge]] for conflict resolution basics, and [[git/stash]] for recovering dropped stashes.
triggers: I committed to the wrong branch
remove a file from git history
revert a commit without losing history
body:
# Git Troubleshooting

Git almost never destroys data. The reflog keeps a record of every HEAD position for 90 days. Most mistakes are recoverable.

## Undo the Last Commit

### Keep Changes (Soft Reset)

```bash
# Undo commit, keep changes staged
git reset --soft HEAD~1

# Undo commit, keep changes unstaged
git reset HEAD~1
git reset --mixed HEAD~1  # same as above
```

### Discard Changes (Hard Reset)

```bash
# Undo commit AND discard all changes (DANGEROUS)
git reset --hard HEAD~1
```

**Warning**: `--hard` destroys uncommitted changes. Use `--soft` or `--mixed` unless you're sure.

### Undo Multiple Commits

```bash
# Undo last 3 commits, keep changes
git reset --soft HEAD~3

# Reset to a specific commit
git reset --soft abc1234
```

## Revert (Safe Undo for Pushed Commits)

Revert creates a new commit that undoes a previous commit — safe for shared branches:

```bash
# Revert the last commit
git revert HEAD

# Revert a specific commit
git revert abc1234

# Revert without auto-committing (inspect first)
git revert --no-commit abc1234

# Revert a merge commit (specify which parent to keep)
git revert -m 1 <merge-commit-sha>
```

**Use revert (not reset) for commits that have been pushed.** Reset rewrites history; revert adds history.

## Reflog — Your Safety Net

The reflog records every HEAD position. Use it to find "lost" commits:

```bash
# Show reflog
git reflog

# Show reflog with dates
git reflog --date=relative

# Example output:
abc1234 HEAD@{0}: reset: moving to HEAD~1
def5678 HEAD@{1}: commit: feat: add auth module
ghi9012 HEAD@{2}: checkout: moving from main to feature
```

### Recover a Lost Commit

```bash
# Find the commit in reflog
git reflog

# Reset back to it
git reset --hard def5678

# Or create a branch pointing to it
git branch recovered-work def5678
```

### Recover a Deleted Branch

```bash
# Find where the branch pointed
git reflog | grep "checkout: moving from deleted-branch"

# Recreate the branch
git branch deleted-branch abc1234
```

### Recover a Dropped Stash

```bash
# Find stash commits in reflog
git fsck --no-reflog | grep commit
# or
git log --oneline --all --walk-reflogs | grep "WIP on"

# Apply the found commit
git stash apply <sha>
```

## Committed to the Wrong Branch

### Move Last Commit to Another Branch

```bash
# Save the commit hash
git log --oneline -1
# abc1234 feat: add auth

# Move to the correct branch
git checkout correct-branch
git cherry-pick abc1234

# Remove from wrong branch
git checkout wrong-branch
git reset --hard HEAD~1
```

### Move Uncommitted Changes to Another Branch

```bash
# Option 1: Stash and apply
git stash
git checkout other-branch
git stash pop

# Option 2: If branches don't conflict, just switch
git switch other-branch  # uncommitted changes come along
```

## Cherry-Pick

Apply a specific commit from any branch:

```bash
# Pick a single commit
git cherry-pick abc1234

# Pick multiple commits
git cherry-pick abc1234 def5678

# Pick a range
git cherry-pick abc1234..def5678

# Pick without committing (stage only)
git cherry-pick --no-commit abc1234

# Abort a conflicted cherry-pick
git cherry-pick --abort
```

## Fix Detached HEAD

Detached HEAD means you're not on any branch:

```bash
# Check if detached
git status  # "HEAD detached at abc1234"

# Save work by creating a branch
git checkout -b save-my-work

# Or return to a branch (losing any new commits in detached state)
git checkout main
# Warning: commits made in detached HEAD are orphaned if you don't branch
```

## Remove Sensitive Files from History

```bash
# Remove a file from all history (git 2.24+)
git filter-repo --path secrets.env --invert-paths

# Or with BFG Repo-Cleaner (faster)
bfg --delete-files secrets.env
git reflog expire --expire=now --all
git gc --prune=now --aggressive

# Force push the cleaned history
git push --force-with-lease --all
```

**After removing secrets**: rotate all exposed credentials immediately.

## Clean Up Working Directory

```bash
# See what would be deleted
git clean -n

# Delete untracked files
git clean -f

# Delete untracked files and directories
git clean -fd

# Delete untracked and ignored files
git clean -fdx

# Interactive clean
git clean -i
```

## Undo a Bad Merge

```bash
# If not yet pushed: reset to before the merge
git reset --hard HEAD~1  # if merge was the last commit

# If already pushed: revert the merge commit
git revert -m 1 <merge-commit-sha>
```

## Undo a Bad Rebase

```bash
# Find pre-rebase HEAD in reflog
git reflog
# Look for the entry before "rebase (start)"

# Reset to pre-rebase state
git reset --hard HEAD@{n}
```

## Undo git reset --hard

```bash
# The reflog still has the old HEAD
git reflog
git reset --hard <old-sha>
```

Reflog retains the reference for ~90 days. Unstaged changes that were never committed are gone forever — `git reset --hard` is one of the few truly destructive operations.

## Force Push Recovery

If someone force-pushed over your work:

```bash
# Fetch the latest
git fetch origin

# Check reflog for the old remote state
git reflog show origin/main

# If you have the old commits locally:
git push --force-with-lease origin main
```

## Agent Best Practices

1. **Use reflog first** — `git reflog` is your time machine
2. **Prefer revert over reset for pushed commits** — revert is safe; reset rewrites history
3. **Prefer --soft reset** — keeps your changes; you can always discard later
4. **Never hard reset without checking reflog** — make sure you can recover
5. **Branch before experimenting** — `git checkout -b experiment` is cheap insurance
6. **Check status after every recovery** — `git status && git log --oneline -5`
7. **Cherry-pick for surgical fixes** — move specific commits without merge/rebase

sections.title: Undoing commits with reset and revert
sections.body: `git reset --soft HEAD~1` undoes the commit but keeps changes staged. `git reset HEAD~1` (mixed) keeps changes unstaged. `git reset --hard HEAD~1` discards everything (dangerous). For pushed commits, use `git revert <sha>` which creates a new undo commit. Revert is safe for shared branches; reset is for local-only history.

sections.title: Using reflog to recover lost work
sections.body: `git reflog` shows every position HEAD has been at, with timestamps. It records commits, resets, rebases, checkouts — everything. Find the SHA you want and `git reset --hard <sha>` or `git branch recovered <sha>`. Reflog entries expire after 90 days (configurable via `gc.reflogExpire`). Reflog is local-only and not shared via push/pull.

sections.title: Cherry-picking specific commits
sections.body: `git cherry-pick <sha>` applies a single commit's changes as a new commit on the current branch. Use for moving commits between branches, backporting fixes, and extracting specific changes. Use `--no-commit` to stage without committing. Use `--abort` to cancel a conflicted cherry-pick. Cherry-pick creates a new commit with a different SHA but the same diff.

sections.title: Committed to the wrong branch
sections.body: If the commit hasn't been pushed: cherry-pick it to the right branch, then reset the wrong branch. If changes are uncommitted: either stash-and-switch or just switch (uncommitted changes move with you if no conflicts). If already pushed: cherry-pick to correct branch, then revert on the wrong branch.

sections.title: Fixing detached HEAD
sections.body: Detached HEAD means HEAD points to a commit, not a branch. Commits made here are orphaned when you switch away. Fix: `git checkout -b <new-branch>` to create a branch at the current position. Or `git checkout <existing-branch>` to reattach (but new commits become orphaned — recover via reflog if needed). Detached HEAD commonly happens when checking out a tag, a remote branch, or a specific commit.

sections.title: Removing files from git history
sections.body: Use `git filter-repo --path <file> --invert-paths` to remove a file from all history. Or BFG Repo-Cleaner for large repos. After rewriting: `git reflog expire --expire=now --all && git gc --prune=now --aggressive` to purge objects. Then force-push all branches. **Critical**: rotate any exposed secrets immediately — git history on forks and caches may still contain them.

sections.title: Cleaning untracked files
sections.body: `git clean -n` dry-run shows what would be deleted. `git clean -f` deletes untracked files. `git clean -fd` includes directories. `git clean -fdx` includes ignored files (build artifacts etc). `git clean -i` for interactive mode. Always dry-run first. `git checkout -- .` or `git restore .` discards unstaged changes to tracked files.

links.context: Interactive rebase for rewriting older history
Bisect to find which commit broke things

related.note: git commit --amend is the simplest undo for the last commit
Interactive rebase rewrites history; reflog recovers from bad rebases
Merge conflicts are a common troubleshooting scenario
Bisect uses log to binary-search for bug-introducing commits
